title: Saag Paneer

overview.description: A Punjabi dish of paneer cooked in a richly flavoured sauce of spinach and mustard greens with ginger, garlic and spices.

ingredients: 200g mustard greens, if available, or additional spinach
method: Bring a large pan of salted water to the boil.
Add the spinach and mustard greens.
Blanch for 1–2 minutes until wilted.
Transfer the greens to cold water to stop the cooking.
Drain thoroughly.
Blend the greens into a coarse purée, adding a little water if necessary.
Heat half of the ghee or oil in a large frying pan.
Add the paneer and fry gently until lightly golden on several sides.
Remove the paneer and set aside.
Add the remaining ghee or oil to the pan.
Add the cumin seeds.
Add the onion and cook until softened and lightly golden.
Add the ginger, garlic and green chilli if using.
Cook for 1–2 minutes.
Add the tomatoes, ground coriander, ground cumin, turmeric, Kashmiri chilli powder and salt.
Cook until the tomatoes soften and the masala thickens.
Add the blended greens and water.
Simmer gently for 8–10 minutes.
Return the paneer to the pan.
Add the garam masala and butter if using.
Simmer gently for a further 5 minutes.
Taste and adjust the seasoning.
Serve hot.

learning.did-you-know: Saag refers broadly to leafy green vegetables, while palak specifically refers to spinach. Saag paneer can therefore contain a mixture of greens rather than spinach alone.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to prepare leafy greens, create a textured vegetable purée and balance earthy greens with rich paneer and aromatic spices.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A classic Punjabi vegetarian dish that introduces the children to leafy greens as the main ingredient and demonstrates how a vegetable-based sauce can be rich and deeply satisfying.
chapman-tips: Blanching the greens briefly helps preserve their colour and fresh flavour.
Don't over-blend the greens if you want a more traditional texture.
Mustard greens add bitterness and depth; spinach makes the dish softer and milder.
If the paneer is very firm, soak it in warm water for 10–15 minutes before cooking.
Don't fry the paneer for too long or it can become rubbery.
A small amount of butter at the end adds richness without making the dish heavy.
The finished saag should be thick enough to cling to the paneer but still spoonable.
notes.batch-cooking: Freezes well. Cool completely before freezing in airtight portions. Thaw overnight in the refrigerator and reheat gently. The paneer may become slightly firmer after freezing.
notes.leftovers: Reheat gently and serve with roti or rice. Leftover saag can also be used as a filling for paratha.
